title: Human Urine on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30–60 minutes home triage; pad bleed-through = pad replacement / restoration
stainChemistry: Human urine on carpet is a protein + uric acid + ammonia stain. The uric acid forms crystals that reactivate with humidity (re-smell on humid days), and the ammonia can yellow synthetic fibers if hot water sets it. Three home traps: hot water (sets protein, yellows fiber, ammonia bonds), saturating the carpet (drives urine into the pad below where you can't reach it and it wicks back as a permanent ring), and chlorine bleach (locks ammonia residue and strips carpet dye unevenly). Wool carpet narrows the bench sharply (broad-spectrum enzymes damage wool keratin) — pro from minute one. Pad bleed-through visible = restoration territory, not a stain job.
whyThisWorks: Cold damp-cloth blot extracts bulk urine without saturating the pad. Cool enzyme pretreat (urine-targeted enzyme like Nature's Miracle, Anti-Icky-Poo, or pet-specific urine cleaner) digests the urea / uric acid / protein at cool temperature. Vinegar disrupts the ammonia residue. Weighted paper towels overnight prevent wick-back from any moisture that reached the pad. UV blacklight in dark to find the full extent — urine often spreads farther than visible.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): human urine is urea + uric acid crystals + salts + protein + sometimes blood (medical). Pro uses urease + protease enzyme blend at controlled dilution + cold acetic acid for ammonia neutralization + sub-surface extraction. Wool carpet requires urease-only (no protease) — protease damages wool keratin.
defaultAssumption: Bedwetting / illness accident / overnight cat-urine event on synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) home carpet. Wool carpet = pro from minute one. Pad bleed-through visible = restoration territory.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on (biohazard, especially if not your urine). Wool carpet = pro from minute one. UV blacklight in dark — urine glows yellow-green and spreads farther than visible. Press the carpet — pad bleed-through visible / musty smell from below = restoration, not stain cleaning.
Damp white cloth with COLD water. Press the spot vertically — never rub. Lift, refresh cloth, press. NEVER pour water — saturation drives urine into pad. Weighted dry towel to extract.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Nature's Miracle / Anti-Icky-Poo / pet-specific urine cleaner per label, COOL water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply urine-targeted enzyme cleaner with damp cloth — NEVER pour. Cover with damp towel to prevent drying. 20–30 min. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1:1 white vinegar + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar + cool water on damp cloth, 5 min. Neutralizes ammonia residue. Damp-cloth rinse. NEVER mix vinegar with chlorine bleach — toxic chloramine gas.
Stack paper towels on the spot, weight overnight. Fan-dry. Re-blacklight verify in dark — persistent UV signal = pad contamination, sub-surface extraction next.

steps: 1. PPE + UV blacklight + pad check — wool / pad bleed = pro / restoration.
3. Cool urine-targeted enzyme pretreat 20–30 min, damp-cloth application.
5. Weighted paper towels overnight + fan + blacklight verify.
products: Nature's Miracle / Anti-Icky-Poo / pet-specific urine enzyme cleaner
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets protein and ammonia
warnings: Wool carpet = pro from minute one
Pad bleed-through = restoration, not stain cleaning
NEVER hot water — sets protein and ammonia
NEVER chlorine bleach on carpet — locks ammonia, strips dye unevenly
NEVER pour water or enzyme cleaner — pad saturation
NEVER mix vinegar with chlorine bleach — toxic chloramine gas
UV blacklight finds urine that spreads farther than visible

homeSolutions.agent: Wool Carpet Goes to Pro — Synthetic Has the Sequence
homeSolutions.instruction: Human urine on carpet has two big risks: (1) the pad below absorbs urine that wicks back later as a permanent ring, and (2) wool carpet's banned-tools list (no broad-spectrum enzymes, no peroxide, no alkaline) eliminates everything that lifts urine from synthetic carpet. Wool = pro from minute one. Synthetic carpet (nylon, polyester, olefin) follows: cold blot → cool enzyme → vinegar → weighted towels overnight. Three hard rules: NO hot water (sets protein and ammonia), NO saturation (drives urine into pad), NO chlorine bleach (locks ammonia + strips carpet dye).

homeSolutions.agent: Gloves + UV Blacklight to Find Full Extent
homeSolutions.instruction: Disposable gloves on (biohazard, especially if not your urine). If you have a UV blacklight (cheap on Amazon, ~$15), turn off the lights — urine glows yellow-green. Mark the full edge with painter's tape. Urine spreads farther than visible. Press the carpet — pad bleed-through / musty smell from below = restoration, not stain cleaning.

Damp a white cloth with COLD water. Press the spot down vertically — never rub. Lift, refresh the cloth, press again. NEVER pour water on the carpet — pad saturation drives urine into the foam below where you can't reach it. Weighted dry towel on top for a few minutes after to extract.

Apply a urine-targeted enzyme cleaner — Nature's Miracle, Anti-Icky-Poo, or any pet-specific enzyme cleaner — per the label, in COOL water. Apply with a damp cloth — NEVER pour. Cover with a damp towel to keep it from drying. 20 to 30 minutes. Damp-cloth rinse with cool water. Cool only — heat damages enzymes AND sets protein.

Mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cool water. Apply with a damp cloth, press 5 minutes. Neutralizes residual ammonia (the smell). Damp-cloth rinse. Never mix vinegar with chlorine bleach — toxic gas.

homeSolutions.agent: Weighted Paper Towels Overnight Plus Blacklight Verify
homeSolutions.instruction: Stack paper towels on the spot, weight overnight. Fan-dry in the morning. Re-blacklight in the dark — if the UV signal is still bright, the pad is contaminated and you need pro sub-surface extraction. Smell test in humid weather a few weeks later — uric acid crystals reactivate with humidity, so persistent re-smell = pad still contaminated.

steps: 1. Wool carpet = pro from minute one.
2. Pad bleed-through visible = restoration, not stain cleaning.
3. Gloves + UV blacklight to find full extent.
4. Cold damp-cloth press-blot — NEVER pour water.
5. Cool urine-targeted enzyme on damp cloth, 20–30 min.
6. White vinegar 1:1 cool damp-cloth, 5 min for ammonia.
7. Weighted paper towels overnight + fan + blacklight verify.
products: Nature's Miracle / Anti-Icky-Poo / pet-specific urine enzyme cleaner
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on wool carpet (pro). Stop on pad bleed-through (restoration). Stop if hot water / steam / hot vacuum was already used (often locks protein and ammonia). Stop if chlorine bleach was used (locked ammonia + stripped dye). Stop if persistent UV signal after full sequence (pad contamination).
warnings: Wool carpet = pro from minute one
NEVER hot water — sets protein and ammonia
NEVER pour — pad saturation drives urine into foam
NEVER chlorine bleach — locks ammonia + strips dye
NEVER mix vinegar with chlorine — toxic chloramine gas

neverDo: Never wool carpet at home — pro from minute one
Never hot water on urine + carpet
Never pour water on carpet — pad saturation
Never chlorine bleach on carpet — locks ammonia + strips dye
Never mix vinegar with chlorine — toxic chloramine gas
Never bare-handed if not your urine
fiberSensitivities: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet: tolerates urine-targeted enzyme + vinegar damp-cloth
Wool carpet: pro from minute one — broad-spectrum enzymes damage wool keratin, peroxide yellows wool
Carpet pad: cannot be cleaned in place — saturation = pad replacement
Uric acid crystals reactivate with humidity — re-smell test a few weeks later

materialWarnings: Wool carpet = pro from minute one
No pour-on water — pad saturation drives urine into foam
Uric acid reactivates with humidity — persistent UV signal = pad contamination
Aged set urine has the wick-back ring problem

escalation.when: Wool carpet from minute one. Pad bleed-through visible / musty smell from below (restoration). Hot water / steam / hot vacuum already used (often locks protein + ammonia). Chlorine bleach already used (locked ammonia + stripped dye). Persistent UV signal after full sequence (pad contamination). Recurring re-smell on humid days (pad replacement).
escalation.whatToTell: "Human urine on [synthetic / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / days / weeks], home-treated with [PPE + UV blacklight + cold damp-cloth blot + cool urine-targeted enzyme on damp cloth + vinegar damp-cloth + weighted towels overnight]. No hot, no pour, no chlorine. [Residual UV signal / pad bleed-through suspected / re-smell on humid days]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with truck-mount sub-surface extraction; specialty wool carpet cleaner if wool; mold remediation specialist if pad contamination + recurring smell

contextQuestions: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet or wool?
Fresh, overnight, days, or weeks old?
Has hot water / steam / hot vacuum been used?
Has chlorine bleach been used (locked ammonia)?
Does the pad below feel damp or smell musty?
Re-smell on humid days (uric acid reactivation)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Wool carpet protocol is pro because broad-spectrum enzymes damage the keratin. Pad bleed-through is the silent failure mode that turns a stain job into restoration. UV blacklight is genuinely useful — urine often spreads 2–3× the visible area. Persistent re-smell on humid days means uric acid crystals are still in the pad.
